optimizer_switches.name: index_merge
optimizer_switches.explanation: Uses two or more index range scans on the same table and combines row IDs (union/intersection/sort_union) instead of falling back to a full scan. A composite index is usually faster.

optimizer_switches.name: index_merge_intersection
optimizer_switches.explanation: Intersection variant: AND'ed predicates are satisfied by scanning each index and intersecting row IDs — useful when no single composite index covers both predicates.

executive_summary: Query runs 4 operators; returns 0 rows in 0.06 ms. No warnings.

query.raw: /* select#1 */ select `testdb`.`t1`.`id` AS `id`,`testdb`.`t1`.`a` AS `a`,`testdb`.`t1`.`b` AS `b`,`testdb`.`t1`.`c` AS `c`,`testdb`.`t1`.`d` AS `d` from `testdb`.`t1` where ((`testdb`.`t1`.`b` = 100) and (`testdb`.`t1`.`a` = 50))
query.beautified:
select testdb.t1.id AS id,testdb.t1.a AS a,testdb.t1.b AS b,testdb.t1.c AS c,testdb.t1.d AS d
from testdb.t1
where ((testdb.t1.b = 100) and (testdb.t1.a = 50))

operator_complexities.folded_label: Intersect rows sorted by row ID starts=1 rows=0

complexity.rationale: Index merge (intersection): scans each index separately and intersects the sorted row-ID streams.
Index range scan: one B+tree descent to the range start, then a sequential walk over k matching index entries.
Index range scan: one B+tree descent to the range start, then a sequential walk over k matching index entries.
